method3.generated_article:
==Geography and ecology==
Jarvis Island lies in the central equatorial Pacific Ocean, about 40 km (25 mi) south of the equator at 0°22′S 160°01′W.[2] It is a small, uninhabited coral island in the central Line Islands, also described as part of the Teraina Islands.[2] The island is the exposed crest of an ancient coral reef cap over a massive extinct volcano, and its center is a dried former lagoon containing deep guano deposits.[2] The emergent land area is about 1,273 acres, while the surrounding submerged lands and waters within the three-mile refuge boundary cover about 36,214 acres.[3]

The island has a tropical desert climate marked by high daytime temperatures, steady easterly trade winds, intense equatorial sun, and scant rainfall of less than 1 m (40 in) per year.[2] Its terrain is mostly sandy and low-lying, reaching only about 7 m (23 ft) at its highest point.[2] Jarvis has no known natural freshwater lens and supports no plants larger than shrubs.[2] Its sparse vegetation consists mainly of bunch grasses, vines, and low-growing shrubs that provide nesting, roosting, and shelter habitat for seabirds, shorebirds, and land hermit crabs.[2]

Jarvis supports more nesting seabirds than any other island in the U.S. tropical Pacific Islands.[2] The island has been described as having 14 species of breeding seabirds, and Jarvis alone has been estimated to support about 1 million sooty terns.[1][3] The island’s ecological importance is heightened by its relative isolation and limited human disturbance, which have allowed terrestrial, coral reef, and deep marine processes to continue with comparatively little interference.[3]

The surrounding marine ecosystem is exceptionally productive because of wind-driven equatorial upwelling and topographic upwelling around the island.[2] Shallow benthic habitats include a fringing reef, shallow back reefs, a steep forereef, and a moderate-sized reef terrace on the eastern windward side.[2] The fringing reef has been characterized as a healthy coral community, in part because of Jarvis’s remoteness and limited anthropogenic impact.[3] Jarvis is also considered an important site for studying long-term climate change and periodic oceanographic phenomena such as the El Niño–Southern Oscillation.[3]

==History==
The first known European sighting was on August 21, 1821, by the British ship *Eliza Francis*, owned by Edward, Thomas, and William Jarvis, for whom the island was named.[2]  

American whalers and other seafarers later noted guano deposits on the island, and the United States Exploring Expedition surveyed Jarvis in 1840.[2] In 1857, Arthur Benson and Charles Judd, aboard the Hawaiian schooner *Liholiho*, took possession of the island for the American Guano Company under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2][4] Later that year, Commander Charles Henry Davis of the USS *St. Mary* surveyed Jarvis and formally claimed it for the United States.[4]  

Guano mining took place from 1857 until 1879, when the island was abandoned after substantial quantities had been removed.[4][5] The United Kingdom annexed Jarvis in 1889, but did not develop mining operations there.[4][5] In 1906, the island was leased to the Pacific Phosphate Company of London, though mining again was not developed.[4]  

The United States reoccupied and reclaimed Jarvis in 1935.[4][5] On May 13, 1936, President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed Executive Order 7368, placing jurisdiction over the island with the Secretary of the Interior.[3] During World War II, Japanese forces attacked the island in 1942, prompting the evacuation of its civilian colonists.[1]  

On June 27, 1974, the Secretary of the Interior designated Jarvis Island and the surrounding territorial sea out to three nautical miles as a unit of the National Wildlife Refuge System.[3] The stated purpose of the refuge at the time of transfer to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service was the preservation of the complete terrestrial and marine ecosystem, with special emphasis on the island’s large seabird nesting colonies.[3] In 2009, Jarvis Island was also included in the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[5]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
The refuge is administered by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service as part of the National Wildlife Refuge System, whose mission is to conserve, manage and, where appropriate, restore fish, wildlife, plants and their habitats for present and future generations.[2] Its stated management purpose at transfer to the Service was “the preservation of the complete ecosystem, terrestrial as well as marine,” with special emphasis on protecting large seabird nesting colonies.[5]  

The original refuge boundary included the island and its territorial sea to the three-mile limit, comprising 1,273 acres of emergent land and 36,214 acres of submerged lands and waters, for a total of 37,487 acres.[5] The Fish and Wildlife Service later described the refuge as encompassing 429,853 acres, including 1,273 terrestrial acres and 428,580 submerged acres.[2] The World Database on Protected Areas reports Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge as a marine and coastal protected area of 1,744.87 km² in the United States Minor Outlying Islands.[1]  

Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge lies within the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, which was established on January 6, 2009.[2] Management planning for the refuge has included a Comprehensive Conservation Plan and Environmental Assessment prepared by the Fish and Wildlife Service.[4][5] Service guidance for the refuge emphasizes conserving biological diversity, maintaining habitat networks for migratory birds and other wildlife, and protecting threatened or endangered species and their habitats.[5]  

Access to the refuge is restricted and managed for compatibility with refuge purposes; the Fish and Wildlife Service has evaluated limited amateur radio operations through a draft compatibility determination, finding that such activity would not detract from protecting the refuge’s natural character, wildlife, plants, coral reef communities and associated tidal, submerged and marine resources.[2] In August 2024, a Fish and Wildlife Service marine national monuments biology team visited the refuge, the agency’s first trip to Jarvis Island in more than six years.[3]

==Transportation==
Access to Jarvis Island is by ocean-going vessel, and ships are described as the only method of visiting the island.[3] The island is approximately 1,263 nautical miles from the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service management staff in Honolulu, Hawaii, and the voyage takes about eight days on average.[3] Transportation has been provided in recent years by NOAA, the U.S. Coast Guard, and private charter vessels.[3] Scientific and management visits are commonly organized as part of multi-island expeditions to remote central Pacific refuges, including Baker, Howland, Johnston, and Jarvis.[3] Refuge planning identifies the island’s remoteness, lack of affordable and reliable transportation, lack of operational infrastructure, extreme environmental conditions, and landing-related safety concerns as major logistical limitations.[3] Fuel and equipment brought ashore for permitted operations must be transported and stored under strict containment and spill-prevention requirements, including the use of approved fuel containers and secondary containment.[1]

==Military==
In the 1930s, Jarvis Island was included in the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, under which the U.S. Department of Commerce placed U.S. citizens on Jarvis, Howland, and Baker to support proposed weather stations and landing fields for military and commercial aviation routes between Australia and California.[1] The program also had a strategic purpose, as U.S. officials sought to reinforce claims to remote central Pacific islands as a check on Japan’s eastward territorial expansion.[1] The colonists, later known as Hui Panalāʻau, were mainly young men recruited in Hawaii, including Native Hawaiian students, and their experience was later documented in the Bishop Museum film *Under a Jarvis Moon*.[1][2] Physical traces associated with historical military activity include remnants of short channels blasted through the shallow fringing reef to improve small-boat access to the island.[4] Refuge planning documents also note that hazards may remain from earlier human habitation during the guano-mining and military eras.[3]
